Okay, what are communication expectations? All right, these are the... So we have the communication cycles. Now, what if they need to speak with you outside of a meeting, a huddle, or a cycle? Okay, that is what this is for. So I gave you all an example of what we use. So one, we wanna choose the medium that they will communicate with you on and then have it written and known everywhere. Like everywhere you can put this, your handbook, your office, email threads, et cetera. Okay, so an example for that would be, hey, for all questions, concerns, or help you need outside of our current cadence, please be sure to vox me only. I will not respond to inquiries via text, email, Facebook. Understand this is to keep things simple and streamlined rather than having communication lost in another realm of social media. Okay. Two, is setting clear expectations by which they should be using this medium. Is it for questions? Is it for concerns? Is it regarding clients? Is it personnel issues? Are they going to email you about something or are they going to vox you about something? What deems necessary of each? And then lastly is to stick with the boundaries. Okay, set your boundaries. Know exactly when you're going to respond, when you're not going to respond. You know, say I only respond between 7 a.m. and 7 p.m. or between 9 a.m. and 5 p.m., whatever it may be, and then stick to it. So what you need to make sure is that somebody reaches out to you in an inappropriate way that you do not respond and you actually respond to that exact question through the appropriate medium, reminding them that that is how they have to reach out to you. It is as simple as this. You just need to remember to keep your boundaries. And to be honest, it is not out of selfishness of like, you need it easier for yourself, but it is the fact that if you have too many lines of communication running, then you're going to lose things. And so this is how you run a business. It is not just for you, it is for everyone else.
